# Empirical Strength Ladder

| Strength | Evidence type | Example | What it proves |
|---|---|---|---|
| Very high | Randomized experiment | Muchnik et al. social influence bias | Visible prior approval can causally bias later approval. |
| Very high | Incentive-compatible experiment | NBER fake reviews | Fake reviews can causally shift consumers toward lower-quality products. |
| High | Structural model with observed manipulation | NBER fake reviews on Amazon | Fake reviews reduce welfare and shift sales toward dishonest sellers. |
| High | Large-scale observational network study | Nature Communications bots | Bots disproportionately amplify low-credibility content early; humans reshare it. |
| High | Coordination network method | Rogers & Righetti / Coordination Network Toolkit | Repeated co-action networks surface manufactured attention patterns. |
| Medium-high | Platform-scale enforcement data | Google Search / Apple App Store / FTC | Platforms identify categories of manipulation and report remediation volume. |
| Medium | Investigative reporting | Geese / clipping / trend simulation | Specific campaigns and market language; strong if backed by admissions or documents. |
| Low-medium | Reddit/operator chatter | forum posts | Useful for vocabulary and leads; not standalone proof. |

## Key point
The argument does not need one perfect source. It is strongest when causal experiments, mathematical mechanisms, platform rules, and observed case studies all point to the same signal-corruption mechanism.
